text.title: Advanced Patterns: Data Vault, Cassandra, Time-Series
text.summary: Data Vault 2.0 for auditable enterprise warehouses; query-first design for Cassandra/ScyllaDB; hypertables and continuous aggregates for TimescaleDB/InfluxDB.

section.title: Data Vault 2.0

rule.statement: Use Data Vault 2.0 for enterprise data warehouses that require full lineage, audit readiness, and parallel loading from multiple source systems. The three components are Hubs (business keys), Links (relationships between hubs), and Satellites (descriptive attributes with history).
rule.rationale: Star schemas are brittle when source systems change — adding a new source often requires schema redesign. Data Vault separates the business keys (stable) from the descriptive data (volatile), enabling incremental and parallel loading without breaking existing consumers.

list.item: Hub: one row per unique business key (customer number, SKU); includes load timestamp and record source
Link: one row per unique relationship between hubs; hash key of composite business key
Satellite: one row per change to descriptive attributes of a hub or link; includes load_dts, load_end_dts, hash_diff for change detection

section.reference: Hub, Link, Satellite DDL templates with hash key functions

section.title: Cassandra: Query-First Design

rule.statement: In Cassandra/ScyllaDB, design the table for each query — not for the entity. Define all required queries first, then design one table per query. Duplication is expected and required.
rule.rationale: Cassandra has no joins, no cross-partition queries, and no secondary index support for high-cardinality columns. The only way to query efficiently is to pre-model the data in query shape. A normalized schema in Cassandra is effectively unqueryable at scale.

list.item: Partition key: determines the node that stores the row; must be in every WHERE clause; avoid hot partitions
Clustering columns: sorting within a partition; enable range queries within a partition
One table per query pattern — accept duplication, it is by design
Partition size target: <100MB; avoid unbounded partitions (bucket by time if needed)

section.reference: Cassandra table template with partition/clustering key design

rule.statement: For time-series data in TimescaleDB, create hypertables with a chunk interval matching the query time window. Add continuous aggregates for pre-computed rollups; add retention policies to prevent unbounded growth.
rule.rationale: Time-series data grows indefinitely and is almost exclusively queried in time ranges. Hypertable chunks enable partition pruning for time queries, automatic compression, and retention-based chunk drops without full table scans.

list.item: Hypertable chunk interval: match to most common query range (1-day chunks for hourly queries, 1-week for daily queries)
Continuous aggregates: pre-compute hourly/daily rollups; query rollup instead of raw data for dashboards
Retention policy: automatically drop chunks older than the retention window
Compression policy: compress chunks older than the hot window (e.g., >7 days)
Tag vs field in InfluxDB: tag = indexed dimension (sensor_id, location); field = actual measurement value
